Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered. I'd spend around 26 hours a week studying, but it wasn't like I had a strict schedule or anything. I'd mostly listen to lectures and podcasts while doing other things - walking to the store, cooking, whatever. I learn best by hearing things, so I'd replay audio content multiple times until it stuck. Some weeks I was more focused than others, but I never really had a consistent system.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot. I'd coast along during quieter weeks, maybe just logging in to check discussions or watch a video here and there. Then when deadlines approached, I'd suddenly ramp up and try to cram everything in. It wasn't the smartest approach, but somehow it worked out okay for exams even if my assignments suffered.
Q3: Usually I logged in because I saw something interesting in the discussion forums or wanted to check if there was new content posted. I actually enjoyed the social aspect - seeing what my peers were talking about. Sometimes I'd log in just out of boredom, honestly, scrolling through without any real purpose. Other times it was pure panic when I realized something was due.
Q4: Yeah, checking the discussion boards became kind of a habit for me. I'd log in most days just to see what people were saying, even if I wasn't actively working on assignments. It happened naturally because I found some of the conversations genuinely interesting. The peer interactions kept me coming back even when I wasn't motivated to do actual coursework.
Q5: The discussion forums were definitely my thing - I posted quite a bit and interacted with other students regularly. I also found the video lectures helpful, though I usually watched them at 1.5x speed or just listened to the audio while multitasking. Anything I could absorb by listening worked well for me. The peer support connections I made were probably the most valuable part of the whole experience.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the reading materials and written resources. If I couldn't listen to it, I probably didn't engage with it much. I also delayed a lot of the assignments - my completion rate was pretty bad, I know. The written stuff just didn't stick for me the way audio content did, so I'd always tell myself I'd get to it later.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it involved actual interaction with other people or if I could learn by listening. The peer discussions were engaging because there was real back-and-forth happening. Anything that felt like isolated busywork - filling out forms, reading dense PDFs - just didn't feel valuable to me, even if it probably was.
Q8: Optional activities felt way more appealing to me, weirdly enough. Required assignments felt like obligations, and I'd put them off constantly. But optional discussion posts or extra videos? I'd actually do those because there was no pressure. I think the lack of stakes made me more willing to engage naturally rather than feeling forced.
Q9: Oh absolutely. I'd download lecture audio and listen to it on repeat during my commute or while exercising. I also talked through concepts with classmates outside of the LMS - like actual phone calls or voice messages where we'd explain things to each other. A lot of my studying happened through conversation and listening, not through the platform itself.
Q10: There were tons of times I was actively learning but it wouldn't show up anywhere. I'd be listening to downloaded lectures while walking around, or I'd be on a call with a study buddy working through problems verbally. Sometimes I'd just sit and think through concepts, talking to myself out loud. None of that leaves a digital trace.
Q11: Definitely. I'd download the audio versions of lectures once and then listen to them multiple times over the following weeks. Same with any podcasts or supplementary audio content - one download, many listens. My actual engagement with that material was way higher than my login data would suggest.
Q12: There were weeks where everything piled up and I just couldn't bring myself to do the written assignments. The stress would build up to a medium level, but I'm pretty resilient so I'd eventually bounce back. The hardest times were when multiple assignments overlapped and I had to choose what to skip. I'd stay engaged with discussions but drop the ball on submissions.
Q13: Yeah, when I was tired or overwhelmed, I'd default to passive engagement - just scrolling through discussions or half-watching videos. I wouldn't actually complete tasks, but I'd still be present on the platform in some way. Competing responsibilities definitely affected my assignment completion, but I managed to keep my social engagement going even during busy times.
Q14: Plenty of times. I'd have a video playing but my mind would be elsewhere, or I'd scroll through forum posts without really absorbing anything. Sometimes I'd log in just to feel like I was doing something productive, even though I wasn't really learning. It was like going through the motions without being mentally present.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even knowing they were important. I think it's because they required focused written work, which isn't my strength. I'd tell myself I'd do better on the exam to make up for it. The peer support I received helped me feel less guilty about it, but it didn't actually fix my avoidance problem.
Q16: Deadlines would create these sudden bursts of activity for me. I'd be pretty chill until a few days before something was due, then panic-engage with everything at once. This worked okay for exams since I could cram the audio content, but it really hurt my assignment scores. The deadline pressure was the main thing that got me to actually submit work.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect on me. Good feedback made me feel happy and validated, but it didn't necessarily make me engage more with future work. Critical feedback sometimes made me want to avoid that type of assignment altogether. I responded better to informal peer feedback than official instructor comments, honestly.
Q18: The course structure was pretty flexible, which was both good and bad for someone like me. I appreciated being able to engage at my own pace with discussions and videos. But the flexibility also meant I could easily skip the structured assignments without immediate consequences. More mandatory checkpoints might have helped me stay on track.
Q19: The discussion forums being active made engagement easier - there was always something happening there. The video content being available to download was great for my learning style. What made it harder was the heavy emphasis on written assignments, which just don't work well for how I learn. More audio-based assessments would have been amazing.
Q20: That sounds familiar, actually. I think students might be genuinely interested and participating in the community aspects but struggling with the formal submission requirements. Maybe they're learning a lot through discussions and videos but can't translate that into written work. Or they're using the LMS as a social space rather than an academic one.
Q21: That's kind of my situation in reverse - I had decent activity but my assignment completion was low, yet my exam score was pretty good. I think some students figure out how to learn efficiently outside the system. They might download everything once, study intensively offline, and only show up to submit. The LMS activity doesn't capture actual learning.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of when people are active and what they click on. But they completely miss the quality of engagement and all the offline learning. They can't tell if someone is actually listening or just has a video playing. They also miss peer learning that happens through calls or messages outside the platform.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and connecting with other people around it. For me, it's about the conversations and the moments when something clicks while I'm listening to a lecture for the third time. It's not about completing checkboxes - it's about genuine interest and involvement with the material and the community.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I learned through listening and re-listening to content offline. It wouldn't capture the meaningful conversations I had with peers outside the platform, or how the peer support intervention actually helped my wellbeing even if my mood stayed kind of low. My real engagement was much deeper than my assignment completion suggests.
